title-group.article-title: Perspectives on collaboration between drug courts and MOUD providers: Impact of interagency implementation strategies

article-meta.aff: aDepartment of Family Medicine and Community Health, University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School, Worcester, MA, USA
bSchar School of Policy and Government, George Mason University, Fairfax, VA, USA
cSchool of Global Health Management and Informatics, College of Community Innovation and Education, University of Central Florida, Orlando, USA
dDepartment of Psychiatry, Center of Excellence for Specialty Courts, University of Massachusetts Medical School, 222 Maple Ave, Chang Building, UMass Chan, Shrewsbury, MA, USA
eDepartment of Medicine, Division of Health Systems Sciences, University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School, Worcester, MA, USA
fDivision of Preventive and Behavioral Medicine, Department of Population and Quantitative Health Sciences, University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School, Worcester, MA, USA
gDepartment of Population and Quantitative Health Sciences, University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School, Worcester, MA, USA

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Department of Family Medicine and Community Health, University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School, Benedict Building - A3-201, Worcester, MA, USA. ekaterina.pivovarova@umassmed.edu (E. Pivovarova)

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Effective working relationships between drug courts and community providers are critical to improving access to medications for opioid use disorder (MOUD), but collaboration is often inadequate. We adapted and piloted a package of implementation strategies (Clinical Organizations and Legal Agency Alliance Building; COLAAB) designed to enhance collaboration between recovery courts and MOUD agencies. COLAAB activities included structured interagency meetings, agency tours, academic liaison, and development of local resource guides.

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: After the implementation of COLAAB in three courts, we conducted semi-structured interviews (n = 24) with drug court and MOUD staff to assess the impact of COLAAB on collaboration, communication, and referrals. Data were analyzed using a modified iterative categorization approach.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: Drug court and MOUD agency staff perceived COLAAB as facilitating interagency relationships and referrals to MOUD, increasing MOUD agencies’ understanding of drug courts, improving communication quality, strengthening understanding and trust in MOUD providers and drug courts, and reducing time to referrals to MOUD. COLAAB also increased the MOUD agency staff’s awareness of other community providers and helped build their potential referral networks. COLAAB may have also enhanced drug court staff’s willingness to speak with clients about MOUD and MOUD provider staff’s willingness to discuss clients’ criminal legal involvement.

sec.title: Discussion:
sec.p: Our pilot study provides preliminary evidence that court-MOUD agency collaborations can be enhanced through active learning about one another’s practices and services, participation in regularly scheduled meetings or activities, and the identification and use of resources that support efficient interagency referral processes.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Over 4000 treatment courts nationwide supervise more than 150,000 individuals with substance use and mental health disorders annually (National Treatment Court Resource Center, 2022). Drug court is the most common type of treatment court where individuals with substance use disorders are mandated to substance use treatment in exchange for being diverted out of the criminal legal system or avoidance or reduction of sentence. While participation in drug courts has been associated with a decrease in substance use and mortality, drug courts themselves do not provide treatment (Wittouck et al., 2013; Joudrey et al., 2021; Kearley et al., 2019). Instead, drug courts must collaborate with community treatment partners to connect their participants to treatment and oversee treatment participation. However, interagency collaborations between drug court staff and community providers have been described as inadequate and ineffective by both groups due to insufficient interagency relationships and communications, lack of knowledge, stigma, and inefficient referral to treatment (Andraka-Christou and Atkins, 2021; Pivovarova et al., 2023; Csete and Catania, 2013; Pivovarova et al., 2024; Andraka-Christou et al., 2024).

Historically, many drug courts prohibited the use of Medications for Opioid Use Disorder (MOUD) (Matusow et al., 2013). In the past decade, this changed in response to federal agencies and state laws requiring that drug courts allow participants to receive MOUD, national professional organizations updating their professional standards, and a federal court settlement indicating that prohibiting MOUD in drug courts violated the Americans with Disabilities Act (Andraka-Christou et al., 2022; Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration, 2019). While most drug courts now permit MOUD, fewer than 15 % of individuals in drug courts with OUD receive MOUD (Farago et al., 2023; Marlowe et al., 2022).

Low MOUD rates in drug courts may, in part, stem from courts’ preference for collaborating with agencies with whom they have established relationships (Andraka-Christou et al., 2024; Clark et al., 2024), and these agencies may not offer MOUD. Collaboration between health services agencies and public-serving organizations, such as drug courts, involves working together to address gaps in treatment services, expand available resources, and share information, all of which require trust and effective communication (Brewster et al., 2019).

In some cases, fewer than 50 % of drug courts report collaborating with providers who encourage agonist medications (Marlowe et al., 2022). While inadequate numbers of community providers of MOUD certainly contribute to low rates of MOUD use, research suggests that attitudes toward and beliefs about local MOUD providers also limit court clients’ utilization of MOUD (Andraka-Christou et al., 2024). For example, in our recent qualitative study of factors involved in court decisions about MOUD, courts always permitted client MOUD utilization when requested from a provider collaborating with the court; but courts required a “vetting” process when clients desired MOUD from a provider with whom there was no established relationships (Andraka-Christou et al., 2024). Courts were more comfortable with clients receiving MOUD from collaborating MOUD providers, because the courts felt more confident those collaborators would communicate important treatment updates, conduct regular drug testing, and offer behavioral support services (Andraka-Christou et al., 2024; Ahmed et al., 2022). Therefore, expanding collaborations between courts and MOUD providers could lead to increased use of MOUD.

Barriers to collaboration between drug courts and MOUD agencies include stigmatizing beliefs held by drug court staff about MOUD (Andraka-Christou et al., 2024; Ahmed et al., 2022; Rich et al., 2005; O’Grady et al., 2024), negative beliefs about the trustworthiness of MOUD providers, including believing that MOUD agencies offer poor quality treatment (Andraka-Christou, 2017), and negative and mistaken beliefs by MOUD provider staff of drug courts (Pivovarova et al., 2024). Moreover, drug court staff lack awareness of local MOUD agencies, services offered, or insurance accepted (Yarbrough et al., 2024). Finally, even existing court-MOUD agency collaborations experience poor interorganizational communication, as previously reported by both drug court and MOUD provider staff (Pivovarova et al., 2023, 2024).

To address these barriers and the gap in referral to MOUD treatment from drug courts, we adapted empirically supported implementation strategies (i.e., COLAAB) to improve collaboration between community correction systems and MOUD agencies. The long-term of objective of COLAAB is increased court client MOUD initiation and retention, with collaboration as the mechanism by which these outcomes occur. This article reports the post implementation assessment of COLAAB, using qualitative interviews with drug court and MOUD provider staff about their experiences with COLAAB and the impact COLAAB had on their inter-agency collaborations.

sec.title: COLAAB
sec.p: COLAAB is a package of implementation strategies that facilitates referrals to MOUD by enhancing interorganizational collaborations between drug courts and MOUD agencies with the goal of improving access to MOUD, including increasing the number of referrals and decreasing the length of the referral process. COLAAB was adapted for drug courts from an organizational linkage intervention (titled MATICCE) (Friedmann et al., 2013; Mitchell et al., 2016; Friedmann et al., 2015) designed to enhance MOUD referrals from community corrections. MATICCE decreased MOUD stigma and increased intention to refer clients to MOUD (Friedmann et al., 2015). COLAAB is a twelve-month manualized program that brings together drug court staff, MOUD provider staff, correctional staff, and academic liaisons (i.e., researchers) to participate in structured meetings, develop or improve referral processes and communication workflow, attend MOUD agency tours, and observe drug court hearings. Education is provided through observational learning, including tours and observations, as well as during structured meetings. An academic liaison serves as a liaison between providers and drug courts, including disseminating information and materials. Each site also developed a local MOUD resource guide that includes information about all participating MOUD agencies, contact information, referral documents, and regional resources. COLAAB consists of three phases (see Fig. 1 for COLAAB activities and materials).

As part of this pilot study, COLAAB was implemented in three drug courts, each in a different county in one Northeastern state. Implementation took place between October 2021 and October 2022. The number of affiliated MOUD agencies, or agencies that had staff participate in at least one COLAAB meeting, for each drug court site ranged from three to five (Pivovarova et al., 2024). At least one MOUD agency per court offered each type of FDA-approved MOUD (i.e., methadone, buprenorphine, extended-release naltrexone). Each site also had at least one representative from the local jail.

sec.title: Participants and recruitment
sec.p: To assess the impact of COLAAB, we interviewed 24 representatives from each site (Site A: n = 10, Site B: n = 8, Site C: n = 6) after they completed implementation. From drug court staff (n = 11), interviewee roles included judges, probation officers, court coordinators, and court clinicians. From MOUD staff (n = 13), interviewee roles included program administrators, nurse practitioners, clinical directors, community liaisons, and recovery navigators. Individuals were eligible for interviews if they had participated in at least one COLAAB meeting (though all interviewees participated in multiple COLAAB meetings), were English-speaking, and could provide informed consent. All eligible COLAAB attendees received two emails at the end of COLAAB activities asking them to participate in a research interview. Data collection was complete when at least three drug court staff and three providers from each site enrolled, and thematic saturation was reached. MOUD provider staff received $50 for participating in the interviews. Restrictions on staff payments prevented reimbursement of drug court staff participants. University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School IRB provided research oversight and approved all materials and procedures.

sec.title: Data collection
sec.p: After completing the informed consent, participants completed a one-hour semi-structured interview by Zoom or phone and a brief demographic form. The study PI (EP, clinical psychologist) interviewed MOUD provider staff, and a criminologist (DD) interviewed drug court staff. Interviews inquired about participants’ experience with COLAAB activities and their impact on interagency relationships and treatment referrals. All interviews were recorded, transcribed verbatim, and de-identified. The interviews were conducted within one month of completing the implementation of COLAAB, between September 2022 and November 2022.

sec.title: Data coding and analysis
sec.p: To identify themes, we used a modified iterative categorization approach (Neale, 2016), which is a mixed deductive-inductive process. First, we developed a codebook based on study research questions and a preliminary review of three transcripts by study PI and two Masters-level research coordinators. All transcripts were double-coded and entered into Dedoose Version 9.0 (Dedoose Version. 9.0.17, 2021), with consensus meetings to resolve discrepancies and to discuss newly emerging categories of relevant data. These new categories of data resulted in inductive, iterative codebook modifications. These codes were exported and reviewed individually by EP, BAC, and AB. The researchers independently reviewed and summarized coded data, focusing on consistencies and inconsistencies in perceived impact, including differences in perspectives between court and MOUD provider staff. EP, BAC, and AB then met to discuss their code summaries, using similarities and differences in code summaries, as well as salient key findings to refine a list of overarching themes within and across codes.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: COLAAB impacted interagency relationships and referral to MOUD by increasing awareness of and understanding of local courts and MOUD agencies, improving the quality of communication, improved understanding and trust between MOUD and drug courts, and decreasing time to referral to MOUD but not necessarily the number of referrals from court to MOUD providers.

sec.title: COLAAB increased awareness of and understanding of local courts and MOUD agencies
sec.p: Drug court staff explained that after participating in COLAAB, they now had increased knowledge of local MOUD agencies, services offered, key personnel, and required documentation for referral and interagency communication. For example, one probation officer said, “It was great to learn that certain providers in the community have these types of MOUD.” [ID305, Site B] Others were surprised by the broad range of treatment services, including peer navigation, “for [MOUD agency]…to say…we can provide coaches…for them, for when they leave [the program]. We all said, ‘Oh my God. What a wonderful thing that is.’”[ID314, Court Coordinator, Site C]. Drug court staff also explained that knowing about multiple local MOUD agencies and their services allowed them to better accommodate drug court participants’ individual needs, “…If we had made one connection with one MOUD provider that we could reach out to, that would have been amazing. And to get all those people in the room. Because it provides a number of options.”[ID309, Judge, Site B].

MOUD provider staff believed that COLAAB helped them understand how drug courts operated. One recovery navigator explained learning how drug courts identified and managed participants, “I like that it’s kind of the same people [in drug court], and [staff] really get to know [participants], rather than just everyone who gets a citation or is brought in for possession or trafficking. [Drug court is] not as widespread as I had thought it would be…it helps them be person-centered” [ID308, Site B]. A nurse said they now perceived drug courts more positively, “…It’s changing people’s lives as we [MOUD providers] are…One patient may come in stating that it’s a negative thing. We…have to realize that there’s other patients that it’s definitely helping.” [ID306, Site B].

Interestingly, MOUD provider staff reported that participating in COLAAB also increased their awareness of other local MOUD agencies. A MOUD provider staff explained, “I’m happy that I got to see what drug court looks like and get to know some of the people and some of the other providers.” [ID 303, Site B] A MOUD operations manager explained that knowing other providers “shrinks [the city] for us tremendously. [We can tell patients,]’Hey, you go here for this. You can go there for that.’” [ID 301, Site B] Another services director observed that having information, obtained during COLAAB, made it easier to refer patients elsewhere, “I have four or five [contact numbers]…I use directly from [COLAAB resource guide]. So that was tremendous to have…we’re under the gun sometimes, where we have to schedule.” [ID307, Site B].

sec.title: COLAAB increased the quality of communication
sec.p: Court staff described improved and more frequent communication with MOUD agencies participating in COLAAB. One judge explained how previously it was challenging to connect with providers, “Because most of the time…you would send an email [to MOUD agency] and you’d never get one back, or you’d never get a phone call back. Or we don’t have a release, so we’re not talking to you. That stuff’s all [now] done upfront.” [ID316, Site C] The judge continued that since COLAAB “[Treatment providers]…have a very big, vested interest. Because they’re getting referrals that they may normally not get.”

One clinical director explained how communication with the courts improved, even though they worked together previously, “[The court has] always been a big referral source. But I feel like this is probably the best that communication has ever been.” [ID321, Site C]. Similarly, a court coordinator [ID314, Site C] described that with COLAAB “…We were able to connect with the community resources that we always used but…didn’t have personal relationships…That’s all changed…now we have a nice rapport with all those people that we need things from, and they need things from us.”

Two COLAAB features were especially helpful in facilitating communication: the monthly, in-person meetings and a local resource guide. A drug court coordinator said, “being relational…being able to have interactions with, being able to break down those myths…I don’t know how much better it can get.” [ID313, Site A]. A MOUD clinician believed that personally knowing court staff would make communication easier when such communication is needed: “Certainly, I do feel like…it would be easier for me to know who to call if I did have somebody in drug court…’cuz I know people’s names and faces, and contact information.” [ID303, Site B].

The resource guide included information about local community providers, services offered, office hours, insurance, referral and record release documents, and contact information for all drug court staff. A probation officer explained, “The best tool ever was the resource guide that contains everyone’s contact information…a point of contact for each provider, identified like a direct email, a work cell…especially…where we have a probationer in our office and they’re in crisis. And they’re in need of a detox today, not tomorrow, not in three hours.” [ID305, Site B]. The biggest concern about the guide was how to update it regularly, one court coordinator explained, “Having some way to continue to update it…is really important. Roles change. People leave…while that contact list now is really helpful, in a month, who knows if it’s going to be as helpful if those contacts are not the contacts anymore.” [ID304 Site B].

Court and MOUD provider staff indicated that frequent information sharing improves care for court clients. A MOUD peer navigator explained that regular information from the court helps the agency manage their clients: “[Courts] test more frequently than us, which…just helps to show patterns…to get a glimpse of how much [patients] really are using, if at all…There’s definitely a bigger sense of accountability for the patient when they’re in the program.” [ID308, Site B].

sec.title: COLAAB improved understanding and trust between MOUD agencies and drug courts
sec.p: Our interviewees reported that COLAAB helped them better understand how each of the agencies operated and increased trust. For example, drug court staff believed COLAAB had decreased negative attitudes among their team members toward MOUD, “[COLAAB] has only reinforced the importance of medically assisted treatment, and…we’re talking about it more with our participants. And…if [participants] see in us that we have more information…and more excited about it…it only gets them more interested in it and…in finding out more about it.” [ID323, Judge, Site A]. Another COLAAB activity perceived to be especially helpful were the MOUD agency tours. Drug court staff explained that being able to observe medication dispensation and monitoring helped to dispel concerns about poor quality MOUD care. As reported by a court coordinator, drug court staff were more willing to send court clients to MOUD agencies: “It’s just…that I feel more comfortable sending someone somewhere knowing I’ve met a person there. I’ve seen the place. I know…what…I’m getting someone into.” That coordinator also said, “This sounds horrible, but they were a lot nicer than I really had pictured in my head…’cuz you think like methadone clinic…and you have this picture of what that looks like.” [ID304, Site B].

Drug court staff believed that by better understanding and trusting MOUD agencies, they were more willing to discuss MOUD, “We’re shining a light on [MOUD], as opposed to just kind of slating it in under the rug…We’re talking about people’s ability to access it more. That’s always a first question now. Where before it would be…an afterthought.” [ID322, Court Clinician, Site C]. One probation officer said, “Conversations are definitely happening a lot more often now…Maybe by talking even amongst ourselves, just amongst staff, talking about the different MOUD medications.” [ID305, Site B].

The drug court staff felt that MOUD provider staff had developed a more accurate understanding of drug courts, especially after observing hearings that are non-adversarial. A court clinician said, “That was one of the most important things that we were struggling with, being able to connect us to the providers and also allow them to see how we work. Because a lot of times, drug court has negative view from community or people…because they’ve heard…negative things.” [ID319, Site A]. MOUD provider staff also indicated that COLAAB led to a better understanding of drug courts. For example, a MOUD clinician said, “Nurse took…that patient and was aware of the connection to drug court…She was able to collaborate better with the understanding that this is a team effort…versus an adversary thing.” [ID318, Site A].

Observing drug court hearings was another key COLAAB component that helped providers build understanding of the court and their patients’ experiences. One clinic director described how observing patient interactions with the court team improved their own attitudes toward patients: “When you see people advocating for themselves in front of the judge, even for marijuana, which is always a big topic of discussion, it’s really empowering…it humanizes them.” [ID321, Site C]. That clinic director also believed an accurate understanding of the court system is necessary to help patients: “[I]t was very useful…getting a better understanding of what our patients are experiencing…what their current system looks like for their reentry programs and how we could better assist them.”

sec.title: COLAAB improved networking and helped establish interagency alliances
sec.p: Overall, drug court staff and MOUD staff perceived that COLAAB helped to improve the inter-agency relationships. One court coordinator observed, “It really was not just about collaboration, but I think even a professional relationship. We can call you anytime. If you have an issue, you can call us.” [ID313, Site A]. Court and MOUD provider staff said COLAAB helped them view each other’s goals as complementary. Another court coordinator said, “There is a common goal. We want everyone to…be sober and lead better lives.” [ID314, Site C]. A different coordinator noted, “everybody has the same end-goal when it comes to working with a participant, even though the nitty-gritty of it is a little different.” [ID304, Site B]. A clinic director explained that after participating in COLAAB, the MOUD provider staff started to see their role as not only providing treatment but helping people avoid future criminal legal involvement, “Having this type of relationship…is helping to establish a better way to serve our community…Where we’re not only assisting people get the treatment that they need, but we’re also stopping that revolving door, where individuals are going in and out of correctional facilities without getting their proper needs met.” [ID320, Site C].

sec.title: Participants believed COLAAB led to faster referrals though not necessarily more referrals
sec.p: Drug court staff spoke about how much easier and faster it was to connect participants with services, “I said [to drug court participant]…You need to make an appointment. We can do it now.’ So, I called with him, and [MOUD provider staff] called back right away.” [ID300, Court Clinician, Site B]. The referral process was simplified, as drug court staff had access to all relevant documentation for referral and release of information. “I remember trying to [work with MOUD agency] releases, it was very confusing. They wouldn’t take my release. So having to try to navigate it with the client, I’m not sure here like what we’re supposed to be signing and all of that. So now we have all the releases…and then having the information about what insurances they take, which they don’t, and additional services they may offer, which is really helpful.” [ID 300, Court Clinician, Site B].

While the process may have improved, it was unclear whether overall COLAAB increased the number of referrals to MOUDs, possibly reflecting the short period of time that passed between the conclusion of the COLAAB and data collection. A lack of referrals could also reflect other factors, such as low court case loads. One court coordinator explained, “…Unfortunately, right now, we don’t have a ton of drug court participants…I don’t think there’s been many referrals placed. But…the want and willingness to refer people to different agencies, that has probably increased.” [ID304, Site B] Interestingly, establishing relationships with providers may have enhanced referrals to other treatments outside of those offered by COLAAB participating agencies. A drug court judge said that collaborations established through COLAAB decreased detention of drug court clients: “[Community Agency] has been great about finding…detox right off the bat. So, it stops me at that point from having to detain people and put them in jail.” [ID316, Site C].

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Collaborations between drug courts and MOUD provider agencies are critical for effectively connecting drug court participants to appropriate MOUD treatment. Ultimately, these relationships should expand access to MOUD treatment. This study is the first to describe the impact of a package of implementation strategies specifically designed to improve collaborations between courts and MOUD agencies – COLAAB. COLAAB is a manualized, theoretically informed package of implementation strategies that brings court and MOUD provider staff together for structured meetings, education, tours of each other’s agencies, and the development of a resource guide. The findings of this pilot study of COLAAB in three courts indicate that drug court and MOUD staff perceived COLAAB as having positive effects on their collaboration, including: increased awareness of each other’s agencies, better communication, decreased misconceptions about MOUD and courts, and more efficient referrals.

Both court and MOUD provider staff had consistent beliefs about the positive impact of COLAAB on their collaboration. The findings also indicate that specific COLAAB activities were particularly helpful in improving collaboration, including local guides for treatment providers and services, facility tours and drug court observations, and monthly meetings, which facilitated in-person interactions between providers and drug court staff. Our findings also suggest that COLAAB may have enhanced drug court staff’s willingness to speak with clients about MOUD and MOUD provider staff’s willingness to speak with patients about their criminal legal involvement. While not the intended target of the intervention, we found that COLAAB appears to have increased MOUD provider staff awareness of other treatment providers in the community and helped MOUD agencies build their potential referral networks.

While we were unable to identify effects on the actual number of referrals, one reason being that we did not collect longitudinal data, we observed an increase in intention to refer based on reports from drug court staff, suggesting that COLAAB could ultimately impact court clients’ use of MOUD. COLAAB uses an active approach to changing referral practices by building relationships between courts and MOUD agencies. Given that courts themselves do not provide treatment, such relationships are critical as they are the mechanism by which MOUD is ultimately accessed. The impact of COLAAB is consistent with others’ findings that linkages between criminal legal and healthcare agencies may improve referrals to care (Friedmann et al., 2015; O’Connell et al., 2020; Welsh et al., 2016; Olagunju et al., 2024; Satcher et al., 2024). For example, opioid treatment courts, a specialized pre-plea model of treatment courts designed to provide rapid linkage to MOUD, had higher rates of MOUD initiation than traditional drug courts (Elkington et al., 2024).

Even when healthcare agencies and criminal legal partners agree about the need for a therapeutic intervention, such as MOUD, doing so requires intentional and targeted activities to ensure that communication and collaboration are effective and appropriate for all partners involved. Other studies have also found that collaboration between criminal legal partners (e.g., jails) and community providers is inherently complicated, presenting with multi-level barriers, such as lack of understanding about each other’s service structure, inadequate communication, limited staffing, or availability of services (Pivovarova et al., 2025; Cooper et al., 2016; Sutter-Barrett et al., 2025). Our study indicates that participants felt that COLAAB increased the perceptions of shared goals between drug court and MOUD provider staffs, despite underlying differences in organization culture and processes.

Our findings also indicate that treatment agencies within the same local area are disconnected from one another. Fortunately, our study suggests that at least some SUD providers are very interested in forging connections with each other, even if an external entity, such as a research team, is needed to facilitate the process. COLAAB seeks to increase the number of court-MOUD agency relationships and to improve the referral process within those relationships to offer court participants all MOUD treatment options, while also decreasing stigma. Future research could explore how drug courts navigate multiple and simultaneous treatment-provider relationships, and also directly measure how those relationships impact referral rates.

While this pilot project offers promising findings about what happens when implementation strategies are used to enhance collaborations, they should be contextualized within its limitations. First, the findings only reflect the immediate time period following court and MOUD provider staff’s participation in COLAAB. Future research is needed to assess the sustainability and perceptions of COLAAB over time. Relatedly, while interviewees believed COLAAB would improve referral efficiency and frequency, few interviewees were able to comment on whether COLAAB had in fact improved referral efficiency and frequency. Second, drug court and MOUD agencies self-selected into the study. Therefore, court and MOUD provider who participated in the study and post implementation interviews may be more motivated than the typical person in that role to forge collaborations and address barriers to collaboration. Also, our study was a pilot in one northeastern state with high MOUD resources, universal insurance coverage, low incarceration rates, and requirements by the state agency overseeing drug courts that all MOUD services be considered for participants. Arguably, this would suggest that these connections between agencies may be better established and operational compared to other areas in the United States. Yet our study indicated improvements in organizational services outcomes across sites, roles, and agencies.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Despite the importance of collaborations between drug courts and treatment agencies in the drug court model, few studies have examined how to improve collaborations with MOUD agencies. Collaborations between courts and MOUD agencies may be a precondition for routine referrals to MOUD treatment, which is currently underused by court clients. This pilot study provides preliminary evidence that court-MOUD agency collaborations can be improved through active learning about one another’s practices and services, participation in regularly scheduled meetings or activities, and identification and use of resources that could support referrals to fast and efficient interagency services.

ack.title: Funding
ack.p: This research was supported by a National Institute of Drug Abuse K23 Career Development Award and supplement (K23DA049953, K23DA049953–02S1) to Dr. Pivovarova. The contents of this publication are solely the responsibility of the authors and do not necessarily represent the official views of the National Institutes of Health, participating sites, or any affiliated institutions

fn.p: None of the authors have any conflicts of interest to report. The views expressed in this article are those of these authors only and do not reflect the position or policies of any affiliated agencies.

Stephenie C. Lemon: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Friedmann Peter: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Alexandra Boland: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Barbara Andraka-Christou: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Taxman Faye: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ekaterina Pivovarova: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. David Smelson: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Dara Drawbridge: Writing – review & editing, Investigation.

app.label: Appendix A.
app.title: COREQ (COnsolidated criteria for REporting Qualitative research) Checklist

tr.td: Which author/s conducted the interview or focus group?
What were the researcher’s credentials? E.g., PhD, MD
What was their occupation at the time of the study?
Was the researcher male or female?
What experience or training did the researcher have?
15 + years of clinical interviewing in criminal legal settings
Was a relationship established prior to study commencement?
What did the participants know about the researcher? e.g., personal goals, reasons for doing the research
Interviewers introduced themselves, institutional roles and professions.
What characteristics were reported about the interviewer/facilitator? e.g., Bias, assumptions, reasons and interests in the research topic
The interviewers provided a brief overview for study, including reasons for conducting this research as described in the informed consent.
What methodological orientation was stated to underpin the study? e.g., grounded theory, discourse analysis, ethnography, phenomenology, content analysis

Study design, codebook, and analyses were guided by the CFIR framework. Emergent themes were derived using a data-driven thematic coding scheme iteratively developed by the analytical team in keeping with grounded theory. We used the CFIR framework and past research to develop the semi-structured questionnaire.
How were participants selected? e.g., purposive, convenience, consecutive, snowball
We used a purposive sampling frame to recruit interviewees who were involved in collaborating with drug courts about provision of MOUD. Recruitment detailed in the methods sections.
How were participants approached? e.g. face-to-face, telephone, mail, email
Individuals were recruited via email and phone-based outreach and interviews were conducted via videoconference.
How many participants were in the study?
Interviews were conducted with 11 drug court staff and 13 staff from community-based MOUD treatment programs in one Northeastern state.
How many people did not wantto participate? Reasons?
Where was the data collected? e.g., home, clinic, workplace
All interviewees were asked to be in private spaces during study interviews (either home or work offices).
Was anyone else present besides the participants and researchers?
What are the important characteristics of the sample? e.g., demographic data, date

Interviews were conducted with 13 providers for medications for opioid use disorder (MOUD) and 11 Drug Court staff from one northeastern US state. Data was collected from September to December 2022. Participants included Judges, clinicians, senior administrators or directors, case managers or coordinators and probation officers.
Were questions, prompts, guides provided by the authors? Was it pilot tested?

The semi-structured interview was reviewed by experts in the field prior study start. Sample questions included: Tell me about your experience of working with researchers and Medication for Opioid Use Disorder (MOUD) treatment providers in improving collaboration, what was most or least helpful? There were no formal revisions to the questions, however some phrasing changed throughout the study for clarification.
Were repeat interviews carried out? If yes, how many?
Did the research use audio or visual recording to collect the data?
Secure audio recording device was used.
Were field notes made during and/or after the interview or focus group?
What was the duration of the interviews or focus group?
The sample size enabled achievement of thematic saturation by main topics.
Were transcripts returned to participants for comment and/or correction?
How many data coders coded the data?
Did authors provide a description of the coding tree?
Codes (categorized data extracts from the interview) were refined using open coding and constant comparative methods, resulting in a coding tree ultimately leading to 24 codes.
Were themes identified in advance or derived from the data?

Emergent themes (patterns of responses from multiple respondents that may span more than one code) were derived using a data-driven thematic scheme iteratively developed by the analytical team in keeping with grounded theory and CFIR framework.
What software, if applicable, was used to manage the data?
Did participants provide feedback on the findings?
Participants did not provide direct feedback on the findings. Manuscript will be shared with the agencies in which the MOUD provider and drug court staff work.
Were participant quotations presented to illustrate the themes/findings? Was each quotation identified? e.g., participant number
Participant numbers and roles were assigned to selected quotes.
Was there consistency between the data presented and the findings?
Were major themes clearly presented in the findings?
Is there a description of diverse cases or discussion of minor themes?

caption.p: Clinical Organizations and Legal Agency Alliance Building (COLAAB) Phases and Activity Descriptions Overview maybe indicate which are ERIC strategies?.

tr.td: Some college or Associate’s degree or Trade
